perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Disclosee under this Agreement shall expire the earlier of (a) t hree years after the date of this Agreement, or (b) the date the Sale between the Candidate and the Sell ers is completed.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Disclosee under this Agreement shall expire the earlier of (a) t hree years after the date of this Agreement, or (b) the date the Sale between the Candidate and the Sell ers is completed. However, obligations related to trade secrets shall continue indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: This creates an in-text contradiction by stating that the obligations expire after three years or upon completion of the sale, but then adding an exception that obligations related to 'trade secrets' continue indefinitely. This is contradictory, creating uncertainty as to which obligations actually expire. The general obligations are said to expire within the stated timelines but then they are now changed to extend indefinitely when related to trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall in no way constitute a commitment by any person to supply any Confidential Inform ation or enter into any contract in connection with the Sellers . The obligations of the Disclosee under this Agreement shall expire the earlier of (a) t hree years after the date of this Agreement, or (b) the date the Sale between the Candidate and the Sell ers is completed. However, obligations related to trade secrets shall continue indefinitely.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the obligations of the Disclosee expire after three years or upon completion of the sale. The changed text adds a caveat that obligations related to trade secrets continue indefinitely. This directly contradicts the earlier statement about the expiration of all obligations, thus a strong in-text contradiction exists, as indicated by the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: It is further understood and agreed that, without prejudice to any other rights or remedies which either party may have, any brea ch of these undertakings could cause the Sellers and/or its shareholders irreparable injury.
perturbation.changed_text: It is further understood and agreed that, with prejudice to any other rights or remedies which either party may have, any brea ch of these undertakings will not cause the Sellers and/or its shareholders irreparable injury.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that a breach *could* cause irreparable injury, while the change states that a breach *will not* cause irreparable injury. This introduces a direct in-text contradiction concerning the potential consequences of breaching the agreement, undermining the enforceability of remedies tied to such injury and adding more uncertainty. The contradiction lies in the contradictory statement with the term being 'irreparable injury' and its assertion of the absence of that specific injury.
perturbation.contradicted_text: It is further understood and agreed that, with prejudice to any other rights or remedies which either party may have, any brea ch of these undertakings will not cause the Sellers and/or its shareholders irreparable injury. In the event of an actual or threatened breach by the Disclosee and/or its Representatives of any provisions of this Agreement , the Sellers shall be entitled (i) t o seek injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction restraining the Disclosee and/or its Representatives from breaching the terms hereof or from disclosing any Confidential Information to any person and (ii) specific performance and other equit able relief.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that a breach 'will not cause the Sellers and/or its shareholders irreparable injury'. The contradicted text states 'In the event of an actual or threatened breach by the Disclosee and/or its Representatives of any provisions of this Agreement , the Sellers shall be entitled (i) t o seek injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction restraining the Disclosee and/or its Representatives from breaching the terms hereof or from disclosing any Confidential Information to any person and (ii) specific performance and other equit able relief.' The original text said that breach *could* cause irreparable injury, entitling sellers to injunctive relief and specific performance. The changed text stating breach *will not* cause irreparable injury contradicts this entitlement, thus it is a contradiction according to the provided definition for Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Belgian law and the Parties irrevocably agree to submit themselves to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of Brussels.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Belgian law, except for disputes relating to intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of the Disclosee's jurisdiction. The Parties irrevocably agree to submit themselves to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of Brussels.
perturbation.explanation: The initial text specifies that Belgian law governs the entire agreement. The modified text introduces an exception: disputes related to intellectual property are governed by the Disclosee's jurisdiction. This in-text contradiction creates uncertainty about which law applies in specific cases. The original statement establishes that any issues are under Brussels jurisdiction, but the current perturbation makes an exception that now the Disclosee's jurisdiction takes precedence with intellectual property disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Belgian law, except for disputes relating to intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of the Disclosee's jurisdiction. The Parties irrevocably agree to submit themselves to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of Brussels.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that Belgian law governs the entire agreement and the parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of Brussels. The changed text introduces an exception, stating that disputes related to intellectual property are governed by the Disclosee's jurisdiction. This directly contradicts the initial assertion that Belgian law and Brussels courts have exclusive jurisdiction over all disputes, creating a clear conflict and an in-text contradiction. The Contradicted text is the same as the Changed Text, which confirms that the modified text contains the contradiction.
